retrieved_docs.title: Choledocholithiasis -- Staging -- Strong Predictors

retrieved_docs.title: Choledocholithiasis -- Staging -- Very Strong Predictors

retrieved_docs.title: Gallstones (Cholelithiasis) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Right upper quadrant ultrasound has a 90% specificity for gallstones and can detect stones as small as 2 mm, along with sludge and gallbladder polyps. Gallstones can also often be identified on computed tomography scans and magnetic resonance, and are frequently incidental findings on studies ordered for other reasons, but these modalities are not as sensitive for diagnosing acute cholecystitis. Approximately 10% of gallstones can be visualized on x-rays due to their calcium content. A hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid scan uses a radiotracer to assess the function of the liver, gallbladder, and ductal system, and can demonstrate diminished gallbladder function. See Image. Gallbladder Gallstones (Calculi), Common Bile Duct. The study can help distinguish acute or chronic cholecystitis versus biliary dyskinesia, a chronically hypofunctioning gallbladder. Ultrasound findings of acute cholecystitis include gallbladder wall thickening greater than 3 mm, pericholecystic fluid, and a painful response to pressure from the ultrasound probe (Murphy sign). [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Cholelithiasis (Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasound remains the first line and best imaging modality to diagnose gallstones. A systematic review estimated that the sensitivity was 84% and specificity was 99%, better than other modalities. Either radiology ultrasound study or point-of-care ultrasound can be used to detect biliary disease. Several studies in the literature have shown that point-of-care ultrasound by clinicians is accurate and reliable in diagnosing or excluding biliary disease. Gallstones on ultrasound have the appearance of hyperechoic structures within the gallbladder with distal acoustic shadowing. Sludge in gallbladder may also be seen, with an appearance of hyperechoic layering within the gallbladder. Sludge, unlike stones, does not cast acoustic shadowing.  If the following additional signs are noted, suspicion should be raised for acute cholecystitis:  thickened anterior gallbladder wall (greater than 3 mm), the presence of pericholecystic fluid or positive sonographic Murphy's sign. Additionally, common bile duct (CBD) measurements can be obtained by ultrasound, and if increased, can suggest choledocholithiasis. The normal range of CBD is four mm in patients up to 40 years of age, with additional 1 mm allowed for every additional decade of life.  Post-Cholecystectomy patients are allowed up to 10 mm diameter since the common duct become the bile reservoir once the gallbladder is removed.

retrieved_docs.title: Gallstones (Cholelithiasis) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Bacteria that produce biofilms are associated with stone formation. Results from a study comparing gut bacteria in individuals with and without gallstones found different subsets of bacteria dominant in each group. [16] [17] Gram-positive anaerobes are linked to greater amounts of the stone-forming secondary bile acid deoxycholate. [11] An analysis of the genomes of bacteria within cholesterol versus pigmented stones found gram-positive bacteria within cholesterol stones but not pigmented stones. Microflora impact cholecystokinin secretion and immunomodulate mucin genes, which contribute to the nucleation of cholesterol gallstones. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis -- Evaluation -- Etiologic Differentiation
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology directly influences management and the risk of recurrence. Thus, early identification of the underlying cause is essential once acute pancreatitis is confirmed. A biliary etiology should be suspected in patients with a history of gallstones, recent biliary colic, or biochemical signs of liver involvement. An alanine aminotransferase (ALT) level greater than 150 U/L within the first 48 hours of symptom onset has a positive predictive value exceeding 85% for gallstone pancreatitis. All patients should undergo a right upper quadrant ultrasound to assess for gallstones, biliary dilation, or sludge. Endoscopic ultrasonography or magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography may identify microlithiasis or choledocholithiasis if ultrasonography does not reveal a source despite a high clinical suspicion. Timely recognition of biliary pancreatitis allows for prompt therapeutic interventions, including endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography and cholecystectomy.

retrieved_docs.title: Gallstones (Cholelithiasis) -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: When recommending treatment, the authors recommended stratification of patients by symptoms, including time to symptom onset and severity of recurrent symptoms. They note that early intervention in those with symptomatic gallstones eliminates clinical decline and complications experienced during the waiting period and that waiting results in greater costs and increased morbidity. Patient stratification helps calculate the risk of complications from elective rather than urgent intervention. [2] The SECURE trial examined persons with symptomatic, uncomplicated cholelithiasis and compared treatment strategies against the eventual operation rate; it found that some strategies reduced the number of operative interventions by a small amount, but overall were not too effective at pain reduction or overall need for operation. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Cholelithiasis (Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, tests such as endoscopic or magnetic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP/MRCP) are sometimes useful when working up patients with jaundice and dilated CBD or suspected cholangitis, but are usually obtained after an ultrasound. ERCP is an invasive test, requiring the use of contrast dye but also has the advantage of allowing intervention if pathology is found (e.g., stenting, stone extraction, biopsy).  MRCP, on the other hand, is non-invasive and does not require contrast dye.

retrieved_docs.title: Choledocholithiasis -- Staging -- High Risk
retrieved_docs.content: At least one very strong predictor and/or Both strong predictors

retrieved_docs.title: Cholelithiasis (Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for most patients managed conservatively or with surgery is excellent. [11] (Level II)

retrieved_docs.title: Gallstones (Cholelithiasis) -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: In addition, for patients with uncontrolled pain, surgery was more effective compared to merely observing their condition without intervention. When comparing elective to urgent surgery, it was noted that 25% of individuals who underwent elective surgery required additional treatment before their surgery date. No significant differences were found in individuals treated with chenodeoxycholic or ursodeoxycholic acid versus placebo in pain or stone dissolution. A study compared acupuncture versus observation for gallstone clearance or dissolution and found no difference. In the pain management category, loxiglumide, a cholecystokinin-1 receptor blocker, was superior to hyoscine- N -butyl bromide, an anticholinergic. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Gallstones (Cholelithiasis) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: When cholesterol gallstones are colonized with microorganisms, the resulting leucocyte infiltration into the gallbladder mucosa in the presence of bilirubin leads to the formation of mixed stones. There are fewer beneficial bacteria, less bacterial diversity, and more pathogenic bacteria in individuals with gallstones. Gut bacteria impact the production of bile acids. [14] Bateria-Sharma et al isolated bacteria from gallstones and introduced them into synthetic bile-like environments containing varying amounts of cholesterol and calcium carbonate. After 20 days of incubation, they found that the slime activity of bacteria correlated with gallstone formation, and other bacterial properties hastened the nucleation of gallstones. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Choledocholithiasis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Bile made in the liver and stored in the gallbladder can lead to gallstone formation. In some patients with gallstones, the stones will pass from the gallbladder into the cystic duct and then into the common bile duct. Most of the choledocholithiasis cases are secondary to the gallstones passage from the gallbladder into the CBD. Primary choledocholithiasis which is the formation of stones within the common bile duct is seen less commonly. Primary choledocholithiasis occurs in the setting of bile stasis, which results in intraductal stone formation. The size of the bile duct increases with age. Older adults with dilated bile ducts and biliary diverticula are at risk for the formation of primary bile duct stones. Less common sources of choledocholithiasis include complicated Mirizzi syndrome or hepatolithiasis. Bile flow is obstructed by stones within the common bile duct, which leads to obstructive jaundice and possibly hepatitis. The stagnant bile can also lead to bactibilia and ascending cholangitis. Cholangitis and sepsis are more common in patients with choledocholithiasis than other sources of bile duct obstruction because a bacterial biofilm typically covers common bile duct stones. The pancreatic duct joins the common bile duct near the duodenum, and therefore, the pancreas may also become inflamed by the obstruction of pancreatic enzymes. This is termed gallstone pancreatitis. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2252
retrieved_docs.content: Cholecystectomy and common duct exploration is classified as a specific subprocedure within the broader surgical category of استئصال المرارة (gallbladder removal). In the context of استئصال المرارة, صورة الأوعية الصفراوية (imaging of the biliary ducts) is closely related, as this imaging modality is routinely used to assess the anatomy of the biliary tree, guide intra‑operative decision‑making, and ensure complete clearance of stones when a common duct exploration is performed. The integration of صورة الأوعية الصفراوية with cholecystectomy and common duct exploration highlights the importance of combining diagnostic imaging and therapeutic surgery to optimize patient outcomes and reduce postoperative complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1258
retrieved_docs.content: The US scan of the gallbladder is a specific type of Ultrasonography of biliary tract, representing a child examination within the broader biliary imaging category. Ultrasonography of biliary tract utilizes the finding method “ultrasound of common bile duct: solitary calculus,” indicating that this imaging approach can detect a solitary stone in the common bile duct. This relationship underscores that gallbladder ultrasound is part of comprehensive biliary tract ultrasonography and that the technique is valuable for identifying solitary calculi in the common bile duct, which is important for accurate diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1610
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic agents for bile duct patency testing and gastric secretion test diagnostic agents are both classified as “other diagnostic agents in ATC.” These agents are used for specialized diagnostic procedures: the former assess the openness of the bile ducts, while the latter evaluate gastric secretory function. By being grouped under the broader ATC category of other diagnostic agents, they share a common regulatory and therapeutic classification despite serving distinct clinical testing purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 743
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are employed to investigate, access, and treat pathology in diverse anatomical regions, and they are organized under the parent clinical actions “Clinical actions A – G.” Within this category, a partial or complete adrenalectomy—or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy—can be performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and may also include excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Cholecystectomy procedures that incorporate exploration of the common bile duct are represented in several variants: standard duct exploration, duct exploration with cholangiography, and duct exploration combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, with or without cholangiography; these can be executed laparoscopically. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 878
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad category of surgical interventions that are performed to visualize, assess, and treat pathology within a variety of anatomic regions. Within this category, specific methods are defined, each tailored to the organ system or clinical scenario involved. Cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct is a recognized method, and it can be further specified as Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct with cholangiography or as Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct with choledochoenterostomy. A related biliary approach, Choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or removal of calculus—performed with or without cholecystotomy and often accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty—may be included as part of an exploration procedure. Laparoscopic surgery also provides a method for biliary exploration, exemplified by Laparoscopy, surgical; cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct. Cranial

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2685
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of biliary tract is a diagnostic procedure performed directly on the žlučové ústrojí (the biliary system). This imaging technique employs an ultrasound of the common bile duct and is capable of detecting a solitary calculus within the duct, thereby providing essential information for the evaluation and management of biliary pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2662
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic agents for bile duct patency testing and gastric secretion test diagnostic agents are both classified as subcategories of the broader ATC group “other diagnostic agents in ATC.” This ATC group serves as a collective category for various diagnostic substances, and it includes specific agents such as urea C‑13. By grouping these specialized diagnostic agents under “other diagnostic agents in ATC,” the classification system provides a structured framework that links procedures for assessing bile duct patency and gastric secretion to related diagnostic tools, facilitating organized reference, selection, and use of agents like urea C‑13 in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2641
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acids and derivatives for bile therapy are classified as bile therapy drugs, representing a specific category of medications used to manage and treat conditions affecting bile production, secretion, or flow.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1728
retrieved_docs.content: Extracorporeal lithotripsy; urinary is a non‑invasive therapeutic technique used to address the pathology of حَصاة (urinary stones). By delivering focused shock waves to the stone, the procedure fragments the حَصاة into smaller pieces that can be passed spontaneously, thereby reducing the need for more invasive surgical interventions and improving patient outcomes in the management of urolithiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2469
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions are classified as a subcategory of اضطراب السبيل البولي (urinary tract disorder). These nonspecific genitourinary manifestations frequently co‑occur with حصاة حالبية (kidney stone), indicating that patients presenting with such symptoms should be evaluated for possible calculi within the urinary system. Recognizing that genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions belong to the broader umbrella of urinary tract disorders and are often associated with kidney stones helps clinicians prioritize diagnostic testing and management strategies aimed at identifying and treating underlying stone disease.

This indicates that in clinical contexts where stones, the risk of mortality, or ureteral blockage are present, the allowed qualifier should be considered a potential complication, emphasizing its relevance for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2683
retrieved_docs.content: The US scan of gallbladder is a diagnostic procedure that is performed directly on the organ identified as žlučník. Biliary tract disease denotes pathological conditions that are located in the same organ, žlučník. Recognizing that the gallbladder (žlučník) serves both as the direct procedural site for the ultrasound scan and as the anatomical location of biliary tract disease underscores the relevance of targeted imaging in the assessment and management of these disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1219
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures are defined as a broad group of surgical actions (Clinical actions A–G) used to investigate, decompress, or remove pathology across multiple anatomic regions, and each named technique functions either as a primary “method” or as a “possibly‑included method” when combined with adjunctive steps. In the adrenal gland, partial or complete adrenalectomy—or adrenal gland exploration with or without biopsy—performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and when coupled with excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor, are classified as exploration methods. Biliary exploration includes cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration performed with cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty (with or without cholangiography), as well as choledochotomy or choledochostomy with drainage, calculus removal, and optional sphincterotomy; laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration is recorded similarly.
